content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

September 21, 2016

Heather L. Stroud
Deputy City Attorney
City Hall 1200 Carlsbad Village Drive
Carlsbad, CA 92008

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-16-164

Dear Ms. Stroud:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding Government Code Section 1090, et seq.¹ Please note that we do not advise on any other area of law, including Public Contract Code or common law conflicts of interest. We are also not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate.

As required by statute, we have forwarded your request to the Attorney General's Office and the San Diego County District Attorney's Office. We did not receive a written response from either entity. (See Section 1097.1(c)(3) and (4).) Finally, we are required to advise you that the following advice is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor. (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).)

QUESTION

Does Section 1090 prohibit the City of Carlsbad from contracting with Mark Sauer Construction for a maintenance project in light of the fact that the company was previously hired to conduct a historic structure maintenance assessment for the project?

CONCLUSION

Section 1090 does not prohibit the City of Carlsbad from contracting with Mark Sauer Construction for the maintenance project because the company did not exert considerable influence over the city's contract by performing the maintenance assessment.

FACTS

The City of Carlsbad entered into an agreement with Mark Sauer Construction for a term of 60 days and total compensation of $1,980 to perform historic structure maintenance assessment services at the Leo Carrillo Ranch Historic Park. The purpose of the maintenance assessment was to ascertain a scope of work and estimated costs for implementing the project. The scope of services consisted of: a condition assessment, maintenance recommendations, product and material

¹ Government Code Sections 1090 through 1097.5. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-16-164
Page No. 2

recommendations, identification of areas of risk to any structure, inspection and assessment of
electrical wiring and light fixtures, assessment of exterior lighting within the designated special
event space, and preparation of construction and maintenance cost budgets.

Pursuant to the initial agreement, Mark Sauer Construction performed the maintenance
assessment "as an independent contractor and in pursuit of Contractor's independent calling, and
not as an employee of City." As required by the initial agreement, Mark Sauer Construction
prepared a report entitled "Building Maintenance Assessment" which lists 31 issues identified at
Leo Carrillo Ranch and provides a cost estimate to address each issue. The Building Maintenance
Assessment also prioritizes the issues taking into consideration the urgency of the need for repairs.
The total cost estimate to implement the recommendations is $172,973.

ANALYSIS

Section 1090 provides, in part, that "[m]embers of the Legislature, state, county, district,
judicial district, and city officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any contract
made by them in their official capacity, or by any body or board of which they are members."

Section 1090 is concerned with financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests,
that prevent public officials from exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering
the best interests of their agencies. (Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Section 1090 is
intended "not only to strike at actual impropriety, but also to strike at the appearance of
impropriety." (City of Imperial Beach v. Bailey (1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191, 197.)

Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has
a financial interest." (People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates
Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) The prohibition applies
regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. (id. at pp. 646-
649.)

A six-step analysis is used to determine whether Section 1090 is applicable. However, only
step one of the analysis is applicable to the question presented: Is Mark Sauer Construction subject
to the provisions of Section 1090 in light of performing the maintenance assessment?

Under the principal of strict enforcement of statutes prohibiting personal interests of public
officers in public contracts, Section 1090 is applicable to independent contractors that serve in
advisory positions and have the potential to exert considerable influence over the contracting
decisions of a public agency. (See Chadwick Advice Letter, citing Davis v. Fresno Unified School
District (2015) 237 Cal.App.4th 261; Hub City Solid Waste Services, Inc. v. City of Compton (2010)
186 Cal.App.4th 1114; Schaefer v. Berinstein (1956) 140 Cal.App.2d 278.) This application
recognizes that independent contractors, who serve the public temporarily, still "owe the same
fealty expected from permanent officers and employees." (46 Ops.Cal. Atty.Gen 74 (1965).)

File No. A-1-6164
Page No. 3

As more thoroughly analyzed in the Cozad Market Letter, Nos. A-1-6364:

To determine whether Section 1001 applies, the focus must be on the scope of

influence the depreciable asset constitutes or holds; and the depreciable asset's ability (S) to be capable

If you have other questions on this matter please contact me at (916) 322-5600.

Sincerely,

Sheldon, P. Wegener
H. P. Wegener

Editor in Law
Senator Counsel

By

[signature present]

Bodgel
[illegible]

sections.question:
Does Section 1090 prohibit the City of Carlsbad from contracting with Mark Sauer 
Construction for a maintenance project in light of the fact that the company was previously hired to 
conduct a historic structure maintenance assessment for the project?
sections.conclusion:
Section 1090 does not prohibit the City of Carlsbad from contracting with Mark Sauer 
Construction for the maintenance project because the company did not exert considerable influence 
over the city's contract by performing the maintenance assessment.
sections.facts:
The City of Carlsbad entered into an agreement with Mark Sauer Construction for a term of 
60 days and total compensation of $1,980 to perform historic structure maintenance assessment 
services at the Leo Carrillo Ranch Historic Park. The purpose of the maintenance assessment was to 
ascertain a scope of work and estimated costs for implementing the project. The scope of services 
consisted of: a condition assessment, maintenance recommendations, product and material 
1 Government Code Sections 1090 through 1097.5. recommendations, identification of areas of risk to any structure, inspection and assessment of 
electrical wiring and light fixtures, assessment of exterior lighting within the designated special 
event space, and preparation of construction and maintenance cost budgets. 
Pursuant to the initial agreement, Mark Sauer Construction performed the maintenance 
assessment "as an independent contractor and in pursuit of Contractor's independent calling, and 
not as an employee of City." As required by the initial agreement, Mark Sauer Construction 
prepared a report entitled "Building Maintenance Assessment" which lists 31 issues identified at 
Leo Carrillo Ranch and provides a cost estimate to address each issue. The Building Maintenance 
Assessment also prioritizes the issues taking into consideration the urgency of the need for repairs. 
The total cost estimate to implement the recommendations is $172,973.
sections.analysis:
Section 1090 provides, in part, that "[m]embers of the Legislature, state, county, district, 
judicial district, and city officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any contract 
made by them in their official capacity, or by any body or board of which they are members." 
Section I 090 is concerned with financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, 
that prevent public officials from exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering 
the best interests of their agencies. (Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Section 1090 is 
intended "not only to strike at actual impropriety, but also to strike at the appearance of 
impropriety." (City o.f Imperial Beach v. Bailey ( 1980) I 03 Cal.App.3d 191, 197.) 
Under Section l 090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has 
a financial interest." (People,. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates 
Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call ( 1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) The prohibition applies 
regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. (Id. at pp. 646-
649.) 
A six-step analysis is used to determine whether Section 1090 is applicable. However, only 
step one of the analysis is applicable to the question presented: Is Mark Sauer Construction subject 
to the provisions of Section I 090 in light of performing the maintenance assessment? 
Under the principal of strict enforcement of statutes prohibiting personal interests of public 
officers in public contracts, Section 1090 is applicable to independent contractors that serve in 
advisory positions and have the potential to exert considerable influence over the contracting 
decisions of a public agency. (See Chadwick Advice Letter, citing Davis v. Fresno Unified School 
District (2015) 237 Cal.App.4th 261; Hub City Solid Waste Services, Inc. v. City of Compton (2010) 
186 Cal.App.4th 1114; Schaefer v. Beri11stei11 (1956) 140 Cal.App.2d 278.) This application 
recognizes that independent contractors, who serve the public temporarily, still "owe the same 
fealty expected from permanent officers and employees." (46 Ops.Cal. Atty.Gen 74 (1965).) 


As more thoroughly analyzed in the Green Advice Letter, No. A-16-084:  
"To determine whether Section I 090 applies, the focus is on the scope of 
influence the independent contractor holds in advising the public entity. (See 
Chadwick, supra, p.12.) In Hub City, the court held that an independent contractor 
that exerts 'considerable influence over the contracting decisions of a public agency' 
is subject to Section I 090. (Hub City Solid Waste Sen1ices, Inc. v. City of Compton, 
supra, citing California Housing Finance Agency v. Hanover/California 
Management & Accounting Center, Inc. (2007) 148 Cal. App. 4th 682, 693.) The 
court in Hub further stated, 'an individual's status as an official ... turns on the 
extent to which the person influences an agency's contracting decisions or otherwise 
acts in a capacity that demands the public trust.' (Id.)" 
In this case, the City of Carlsbad identified a need for the maintenance at the Leo Carrillo 
Ranch Historic Park and entered into a short term 60-day contract, for less than $2,000, with a 
construction company to identify repairs and prioritize the repairs based upon the company's initial 
cost estimates. While the company's completed assessment will no doubt affect the final 
maintenance contract to some extent, we do not find that the company exerted "considerable 
influence" over the final maintenance contract in merely performing the assessment. This 
conclusion is further supported by the fact that the City of Carlsbad entered into the initial contract 
for a short period of time and for the sole purpose of identifying and prioritizing necessary repairs 
in consultation with a construction specialist. In this case, no interest is served in disqualifying the 
company from subsequently bidding on the maintenance project based upon such a limited role in 
performing the assessment.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does Section 1090 prohibit the City of Carlsbad from contracting with Mark Sauer 
Construction for a maintenance project in light of the fact that the company was previously hired to 
conduct a historic structure maintenance assessment for the project?

CONCLUSION: Section 1090 does not prohibit the City of Carlsbad from contracting with Mark Sauer 
Construction for the maintenance project because the company did not exert considerable influence 
over the city's contract by performing the maintenance assessment.
